claim: Evidence dispatch implements provider-specific tool configuration normalization and boundary enforcement through executionConfig, buildProviderInvocation, and snapshot-based mutation detection.

evidence.summary: executionConfig normalizes provider settings: Claude tool profile becomes research/full/boundary, tool flags are cleared, session persistence disabled, Codex avoids Claude model inheritance, custom gets boundary sandbox.
evidence.why: This is the core provider configuration logic that prevents cross-provider model leakage and preserves tool capability under harness boundary.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime builds boundary snapshots before/after agent execution, uses buildBoundarySnapshot with git ls-files for tracked files and walkFiles for untracked, then diffBoundarySnapshots to detect mutations.
evidence.why: Boundary enforcement is critical for preventing evidence agents from modifying project files outside allowed artifact roots.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot uses git ls-files -co --exclude-standard for tracked files and walkFiles for all files, excluding node_modules/dist/.git/.cache/.next/coverage, then computes SHA-256 digests.
evidence.why: Git-backed boundary detection is the primary mechanism for identifying project mutations during agent execution.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation constructs provider-specific CLI invocations: Codex gets --cd/--sandbox/--output-last-message, Custom gets shell command with rendered ${model}, Claude gets -p/--model/--permission-mode/--settings.
evidence.why: Provider invocation builder is the dispatch layer that translates normalized config into actual CLI arguments.

evidence.summary: selectedProvider normalizes provider value via normalizeAgentProvider and defaults to claude when empty.
evidence.why: Provider selection logic prevents provider drift between Claude, Codex, and custom assumptions.

evidence.summary: Test verifies Codex dispatch does not inherit Claude default model and does not force read-only sandbox; config.ai.provider becomes codex, codex_model is used instead of model.
evidence.why: Test coverage confirms cross-provider model leakage protection.

evidence.summary: Test verifies Claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement; claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools are cleared.
evidence.why: Test confirms tool capability preservation under boundary enforcement instead of restrictive tool flags.

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass; spawnProviderPrompt callback writes unexpected-project-write.txt, boundary.violated is detected.
evidence.why: Test confirms boundary enforcement actually blocks mutations and adds BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers.

evidence.summary: Test verifies git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations; git init, write untracked-mutation.txt, boundary.violated with changes array containing added file.
evidence.why: Test confirms git-based boundary detection works for untracked files.

evidence.summary: Test verifies JSON repair handles minor Claude output drift; extracts JSON from markdown fence, repairs line 42-88 format, escapes backslashes in inventory API schema, removes trailing commas.
evidence.why: Test confirms JSON repair prevents parse errors from minor provider output formatting issues.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents behave consistently with the mocked spawnProviderPrompt behavior in tests.
Git ls-files --exclude-standard is available and works correctly in the target project environment.
SHA-256 file digest computation is fast enough for boundary snapshots on reasonable project sizes.
Provider CLI argument flags (--model, --sandbox, --permission-mode, etc.) do not change between provider versions.
risks: A provider-specific CLI change could break invocation assumptions without test coverage for actual provider binary execution.
Git-based boundary detection fails if .git directory is missing or git binary is unavailable.
File digest computation on large projects could slow evidence dispatch runtime.
JSON repair logic could mis-repair malformed but semantically valid JSON from providers.

result.notes: Evidence dispatch has comprehensive implementation and test coverage for provider configuration normalization, boundary enforcement, and cross-provider leakage protection. Assumptions about real provider behavior vs. mocks remain unverified.
